architecture.summary: RetrievalPRM augments a base PRM (fine-tuned Qwen-2.5-Math-7B-Instruct) with a two-stage retrieval mechanism. At inference, retrieved semantically similar questions and steps are prepended to the input prompt as in-context examples. Question-level retrieval uses Sentence-BERT embeddings with PCA dimensionality reduction and cosine similarity to select top-k similar questions; step-level retrieval then selects similar steps from the retrieved questions' solutions using the same similarity metric. Both retrieved artifacts flow as text into the model's input, treated as a warm-up context that helps the PRM generalize across distribution shifts.
trained_components: base PRM (Qwen-2.5-Math-7B-Instruct fine-tuned on PRM800K or Math-Shepherd with LoRA)

biblio.title: Retrieval-Augmented Process Reward Model for Generalizable Mathematical Reasoning

claims.statement: RetrievalPRM-7B achieves average F1 score of 65.8 across GSM8K, MATH, OlympiadBench, and OmniMATH on ProcessBench
RetrievalPRM-7B F1 score on OlympiadBench is 60.2, compared to Qwen2.5-Math-7B-PRM800K baseline of 50.7
RetrievalPRM (w/o Step-level Retrieval) achieves F1 of 65.0 on ProcessBench average, versus 65.8 for full RetrievalPRM
RetrievalPRM (w/o Question-level Retrieval) achieves F1 of 63.0 on ProcessBench average

RetrievalPRM outperforms all evaluated language models as critics, including Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct and Llama3.3-70B-Instruct, with the sole exception of QwQ-32B-Preview, achieving this with a model size of just 7B

The paper identifies two key OOD issues in PRMs: step OOD caused by differences in reasoning patterns across model types (GPT, Qwen) and model sizes (1.5B, 72B), and question OOD arising from dataset shifts between training data (GSM8K, MATH) and real-world problems (OlympiadBench, OmniMATH)
RetrievalPRM retrieval pool is only constructed from PRM800K and Math-Shepherd, which is relatively small and limits the diversity and breadth of mathematical problems

Using Sentence-BERT to embed questions and steps struggles to capture the full complexity of mathematical problems as semantic similarity does not mean knowledge similarity in math problems; the naive cosine similarity may fail to accurately reflect true similarity between two questions
